coredata.dc:title: A ROS-Responsive nanoparticle for nuclear gene delivery and autophagy restoration in Parkinson's disease therapy

coredata.dc:description: Parkinson's disease (PD) is characterized by the pathological aggregation of α-synuclein (α-syn) and neuroinflammation. Current gene therapies face challenges in nuclear delivery and resolving pre-existing α-syn aggregates. Here, we developed glucose-and trehalose-functionalized carbonized polymer dots (GT-PCDs) loaded with plasmid DNA (pDNA) for targeted gene delivery and autophagy restoration. The GT-PCDs@pDNA nanoparticles exhibit reactive oxygen species (ROS)-responsive behavior, enabling efficient nuclear entry under oxidative stress conditions. Both in vitro and in vivo studies demonstrated that GT-PCDs@pDNA effectively silenced SNCA gene expression, reduced α-syn aggregates, and restored autophagic flux by promoting transcription factor EB (TFEB) nuclear translocation. Moreover, GT-PCDs@pDNA enhanced blood-brain barrier (BBB) permeability via glucose transporter 1 (Glut-1)-mediated transcytosis, significantly improving motor deficits and reducing neuroinflammation in a 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine (MPTP)-induced PD mouse model. This multifunctional nanocarrier system offers a promising strategy for combined gene therapy and autophagy modulation in neurodegenerative diseases.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization and ROS Responsiveness of GT-PCDs@pDNA Nanoparticles. (a) TEM images of CDs, GT-PCDs, and GT-PCDs@pDNA. (b) Hydrodynamic sizes of nanoparticles measured by DLS. (c) Zeta potential of CDs, GT-PCDs, and GT-PCDs@pDNA (n = 4). (d) FT-IR spectra of CDs and GT-PCDs. (e–h) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s and O1s for CDs and GT-PCDs. (i) Agarose gel electrophoresis of DNA and GT-PCDs/pDNA at various mass ratios. (j) Schematic of drug release from GT-PCDs@pDNA post-H2O2 treatment. (k) Particle size changes of GT-PCDs@pDNA with different H2O2 concentrations (n = 3). (l) Zeta potential changes of GT-PCDs@pDNA post-H2O2 (0.8 mM) treatment (n = 4). (m, n) Measurement of particle size (m) and surface potential (n) of GT-PCDs@pDNA in 10 % serum-containing medium and PBS (n = 4). (o) Agarose gel electrophoresis analysis to investigate the release of pDNA loaded in GT-PCDs@pDNA under different conditions. Data are presented as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was determined using t-test (m, n). ns = not significant.

Specificity of ROS-mediated GT-PCD@pDNA for nuclear gene delivery. (a) Schematic illustration of GT-PCD@pDNA nanoparticles crossing the nuclear membrane. (b–d) Representative confocal images of untreated and MPP + -treated SH-SY5Y cells incubated with GT-PCD@pDNA for 1 h (b). Fluorescence intensity profiles along the white solid line in (b) (c). Quantification of colocalization between Cy5.5-labeled GT-PCD@pDNA and Hoechst 33342 (n = 8) (d). (e–h) Confocal images of Cy5.5-labeled GT-PCD@pDNA (red) in SH-SY5Y cells at 0, 30, 60, and 120 min (e). Fluorescence intensity profiles along the white solid line in (e) (f). Quantification of colocalization between Cy5.5-labeled GT-PCD@pDNA and Hoechst 33342 (n = 8) (g). Changes in nanoparticle diameter over time and the proportion of diameter reduction (h). (i) Schematic illustration of gene transfection using GT-PCD@pDNA. (j–m) Fluorescence images and flow cytometry results of untreated and MPP + -treated SH-SY5Y cells after co-culture with pDNA and GT-PCD@pDNA (carrying RFP) for 24 h (j). Flow cytometry analysis of transfection efficiency in (j) (n = 3) (k). Fluorescence images and flow cytometry results of SH-SY5Y cells treated with GT-PCD@pDNA at different concentrations of the model inducer for 24 h (l). Flow cytometry analysis of transfection efficiency in (j) and (l) (n = 3) (m). Note: Panels (a) and (i) were created using BioRender.com. Data are presented as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was determined using t-test (d) and ANOVA with Tukey's post hoc test (g, h, k, m). ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001. ns = not significant. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

GT-PCDs@pDNA Degrade α-syn and Aggregates and Restore Autophagic Dysfunction. (a, b) Immunofluorescence analysis of α-syn clearance after GT-PCDs@pDNA treatment (a). Mean fluorescence intensity of α-syn (n = 4) (b). (c) SNCA mRNA levels in cells treated with nanoparticles (n = 3). (d, e) Western blot analysis of α-syn monomers and aggregates in treated SH-SY5Y cells (d). Quantification of α-syn and α-syn aggregates, normalized to β-actin levels (n = 3) (e). (f) Schematic representation of GT-PCDs@pDNA-mediated α-syn degradation. (g, h) Representative immunofluorescence images of TFEB (red) with nuclei stained by Hoechst 33342 (blue) (g). Quantification of TFEB fluorescence intensity and colocalization with Hoechst 33342 (n = 5) (h). (i, j) Western blot analysis of LC3 and β-actin in treated cells (i). Quantification of LC3-II/LC3-I ratio (n = 3) (j). (k, l) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of cells treated with G-PCDs@pDNA or GT-PCDs@pDNA. Red arrows indicate autophagosomes/autolysosomes (k). Quantification of autolysosome numbers (n = 3) (l). (m) Schematic illustration of RFP-GFP-LC3 color changes indicating autophagic flux. In the acidic milieu of autolysosomes, GFP undergoes quenching, whereas RFP remains unaffected [47]. (n–p) Representative confocal images of cells after treatment (n). Fluorescence intensity profiles along the white solid line in (n) (o). GFP/RFP ratio as an indicator of autophagic flux (n = 6) (p). (q, r) Lysosomal morphology assessed using LAMP1 antibody staining (q). Quantitative analysis of lysosomal parameters: lysosomal diameter increase (%), percentage of cells with enlarged lysosomes, and lysosome number per cell (r). Notes: Panels (f) and (m) were created using BioRender.com. Data are presented as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was determined using t-tests (h) or ANOVA with Tukey's post hoc test (b, c, e, j, l, p, r [lysosome number, percentage of cells with enlarged lysosomes]), or Kruskal-Wallis test with Dunn's post hoc test (r [lysosome diameter increase]). ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001; ns = not significant. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Enhanced GT-PCDs@pDNA Delivery Across the BBB and Efficient Nuclear Gene Delivery in Neurons with High ROS. (a) Schematic of the Transwell-based BBB model. (b) Fluorescence detection of Cy5.5-pDNA in the lower chamber after 12 h. (c) Cumulative pDNA transport across the BBB at different time points (n = 3). (d) Flow cytometry analysis of SH-SY5Y cells in the lower chamber after 12 h. (e, f) Representative fluorescence images of Cy5.5-GT-PCDs@pDNA in SH-SY5Y cells (e). Quantification of fluorescence intensity in SH-SY5Y cells (n = 3) (f). (g) Workflow of in vivo imaging and brain slice analysis. (h) Fluorescence imaging of mice injected with Cy5.5-GT-PCDs@pDNA under fasting (−) and non-fasting conditions (+). (i) Schematic illustration of the mechanism for GT-PCDs@pDNA crossing the BBB. (j) Detection of Cy5.5-GT-PCDs@pDNA in various organs 4 h post-injection. (k) Quantitative accumulation of Cy5.5-pDNA in different organs (n = 3). (l–n) Representative confocal images of nuclear gene delivery in substantia nigra neurons (l). Fluorescence intensity profiles along the white solid line in (l) (m). Colocalization of Cy5.5-GT-PCDs@pDNA with Hoechst 33342 (n = 6) (n). (o–q) Schematic of pDNA delivery and RFP protein expression (o). RFP signals in substantia nigra neurons of untreated and MPTP-treated mice after GT-PCDs@pDNA injection (p). Quantification of RFP expression to evaluate gene delivery (n = 3) (q). (r) TEM images of nanoparticles isolated from brain tissues of untreated and MPTP-treated mice 24 h post-injection. Notes: Panels (a), (g), (h), and (o) were created using BioRender.com. Data are presented as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was determined using t-tests (n, q) or ANOVA with Tukey's post hoc test (c, f, k). ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001; ns = not significant.

Therapeutic Effects of GT-PCDs@pDNA in MPTP-Induced PD Mice. (a) Experimental timeline overview. (b–d) Schematic of the open-field test (b). Representative behavioral trajectories of normal and MPTP-treated mice after administration of different formulations (c). Open-field test metrics: walking distance, speed, resting time, and central area distance (n = 6) (d). (e, f) Schematic of the pole test (e). Pole test results: turning time and descending time (6 mice per group, 2–3 repeats per mouse) (f). (g, h) Schematic of the rotarod test (g). Latency to fall in the rotarod test (6 mice per group, 2–3 repeats per mouse) (h). (i) SNCA mRNA levels in the substantia nigra (n = 3). (j, k) Western blot analysis of α-syn) and β-actin in substantia nigra homogenates (j). Quantification of α-syn and α-syn aggregates, normalized to β-actin levels (n = 3) (k). (l, m) Western blot analysis of LC3 and β-actin in substantia nigra homogenates (l). Quantification of LC3-II/LC3-I ratio (n = 3) (m). (n, o) Representative immunofluorescence staining of tyrosine hydroxylase (TH) and α-syn in the substantia nigra (n). Quantification of α-syn fluorescence intensity, α-syn/Hoechst 33342 ratio, TH + neuron count, soma size, and fiber length (n = 3) (o). (p) Schematic of the immunohistochemistry (IHC) procedure. (q, r) Representative IHC images of TH in the substantia nigra (q). Quantification of TH-positive cells (n = 3) (r). Notes: Panels (a), (b), (e), (g), and (p) were created using BioRender.com. For behavioral analyses (e–h), increased replicates per mouse were included to account for inherent variability and enhance statistical robustness. Data are presented as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was analyzed using one-way ANOVA with Bonferroni post hoc test (d, f, h, o) or Tukey post hoc test (i, k, m, r). ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001; ns = not significant.

GT-PCDs@pDNA Modulate the Neuroimmune Microenvironment in MPTP-Induced Parkinson's Disease Mice. (a, b) Representative immunofluorescence images of microglial marker Iba1 in the substantia nigra of normal and MPTP-treated mice after different treatments (a). Quantitative analysis of Iba1 fluorescence intensity and morphological parameters (cell body diameter, junctions, and process length) using ImageJ (3 mice per group, 5–8 cells per mouse) (b). (c) Schematic of GT-PCDs@pDNA-mediated neuronal recovery (created using BioRender.com). (d) ELISA analysis of inflammatory cytokines in brain tissues (n = 3). Notes: Data are presented as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was determined using one-way ANOVA with Tukey's post hoc test (b, d). ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001; ns = non-significant.

Schematic Illustration of GT-PCDs@pDNA Preparation and Its Mechanism for Reducing α-Syn. a) Preparation of GT-PCDs@pDNA. b) In the treatment of PD, the combination of ROS-responsive GT-PCDs@pDNA nuclear gene delivery with trehalose-induced autophagy restoration in PD treatment, along with target gene silencing for therapeutic purposes. Upon MPTP treatment, ROS triggers the cleavage of thioketal bonds, releasing trehalose. Trehalose promotes nuclear localization of TFEB, restoring the autophagy-lysosome pathway, and accelerating degradation of α-syn aggregates. Concurrently, ROS responsiveness facilitates nanoparticle entry into the nucleus and pDNA release, enabling successful transcription of exogenous genes and silencing of SNCA, thereby inhibiting α-syn expression. Ultimately, trehalose and pDNA synergistically clear existing aggregates and prevent their regeneration, achieving therapeutic objectives. Created with BioRender.com.

head.ce:title: A ROS-Responsive nanoparticle for nuclear gene delivery and autophagy restoration in Parkinson's disease therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Parkinson's disease (PD) is characterized by the pathological aggregation of α-synuclein (α-syn) and neuroinflammation. Current gene therapies face challenges in nuclear delivery and resolving pre-existing α-syn aggregates. Here, we developed glucose-and trehalose-functionalized carbonized polymer dots (GT-PCDs) loaded with plasmid DNA (pDNA) for targeted gene delivery and autophagy restoration. The GT-PCDs@pDNA nanoparticles exhibit reactive oxygen species (ROS)-responsive behavior, enabling efficient nuclear entry under oxidative stress conditions. Both in vitro and in vivo studies demonstrated that GT-PCDs@pDNA effectively silenced SNCA gene expression, reduced α-syn aggregates, and restored autophagic flux by promoting transcription factor EB (TFEB) nuclear translocation. Moreover, GT-PCDs@pDNA enhanced blood-brain barrier (BBB) permeability via glucose transporter 1 (Glut-1)-mediated transcytosis, significantly improving motor deficits and reducing neuroinflammation in a 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine (MPTP)-induced PD mouse model. This multifunctional nanocarrier system offers a promising strategy for combined gene therapy and autophagy modulation in neurodegenerative diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Parkinson's disease (PD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by the pathological accumulation of α-synuclein (α-syn) [1]. Reducing α-syn levels has emerged as a promising therapeutic strategy [2], with gene therapy showing significant potential in downregulating α-syn expression [3]. Recent advances in RNA interference (RNAi) technologies have yielded three principal gene-silencing modalities: small interfering RNA (siRNA), microRNA (miRNA), and short hairpin RNA (shRNA), each with distinct mechanistic profiles [4–6]. siRNA and miRNA operate primarily through cytoplasmic RNA-induced silencing complexes (RISC) without nuclear involvement. Synthetic siRNA employs perfect sequence complementarity to direct target mRNA cleavage, whereas endogenous miRNA mediates post-transcriptional regulation through partial base pairing, enabling simultaneous modulation of multiple gene networks [7]. Both modalities, however, face pharmacokinetic limitations including rapid nuclease degradation and transient effects requiring repeated administration [8]. In contrast, shRNA transcends these limitations through vector-mediated expression. Transcribed as precursor RNAs in the nucleus, shRNA transcripts undergo Exportin-5-mediated cytoplasmic export followed by Dicer processing into functional siRNA analogues, enabling sustained target suppression [9]. This nuclear transcription requirement, while conferring pharmacokinetic advantages, introduces unique delivery challenges - particularly for neurological targets where the blood-brain barrier and neuronal nuclear membrane pose dual obstacles. Our focus on engineered nanovectors addresses this critical need by synergizing three essential properties: (1) blood-brain barrier penetrance, (2) nuclear localization capability, and (3) cell specific targeting, thereby advancing the translational potential of α-syn-directed gene therapies [10–12] (see Scheme 1 ).

While gene therapy inhibits the synthesis of new α-syn, it does not address pre-formed aggregates [13], which impair selective autophagy [14], a key mechanism for clearing pathological protein aggregates in PD [15]. Autophagy dysfunction exacerbates α-syn aggregation, creating a vicious cycle [16]. Trehalose (Tre) has been shown to modulate autophagy and promote α-syn degradation [17], but its therapeutic potential is hindered by limited brain delivery, rapid metabolism, and low bioavailability due to the lack of mammalian trehalose-specific transporters at the blood-brain barrier (BBB) [18–20]. Overcoming the BBB is a critical challenge for effective brain-targeted delivery [3,21,22]. Glucose, the brain's primary energy source [23], crosses the BBB via glucose transporter-1 (Glut-1) [24]. Modulating Glut-1 activity through glycemic control has shown promise in enhancing glycosylated nanomedicine delivery across the BBB [21,25,26].

This study introduces a novel nanoparticle system designed to inhibit and clear α-syn aggregates through three key mechanisms: (1) nuclear delivery of therapeutic DNA, (2) restoration of autophagic function, and (3) efficient BBB penetration. Carbonized polymer dots (CDs) were synthesized via a hydrothermal method to produce ultra-small, positively charged particles with surface carboxyl groups, enabling nuclear entry. The CDs were functionalized with glucose (Glu) and trehalose (Tre) using polyethylene glycol (PEG) and reactive oxygen species (ROS)-sensitive thioketal linkers. pDNA was loaded onto the CDs through electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions, forming Glu-Tre-PEG-CDs@pDNA (GT-PCDs@pDNA) nanoparticles. The glucose modification enhanced BBB penetration, while elevated ROS in PD pathology triggered the degradation of thioketal linkers, releasing trehalose and reducing nanoparticle size for efficient nuclear delivery. This system effectively silenced SNCA gene expression and restored autophagy, alleviating α-syn-induced neurotoxicity and improving PD pathology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and ROS-responsive evaluation of GT-PCDs@pDNA nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, CDs were synthesized using microwave and hydrothermal methods. Surface modification with Glu-PEG and Tre-PEG yielded GT-PCDs, as detailed in Scheme S1 and Fig. S1. Particle size and Zata-potential were characterized via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and a laser particle size analyzer (Fig. 1 a and b). The CDs exhibited an average particle size of 4.2 nm (Fig. 1b) and a Zeta-potential of +27.4 mV (Fig. 1c). After grafting Glu-Tre-PEG, the size increased to 38.1 nm, and the Zeta-potential decreased to +19.4 mV. When loaded with pDNA to form GT-PCDs@pDNA, the size remained similar, but the Zeta-potential decreased further to −1.7 mV.

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy confirmed the chemical composition (Fig. 1d). The –CONH absorption band at 1351 cm−1 indicated dehydration polymerization during hydrothermal synthesis [27], while peaks at 1710 cm−1 and 1250 cm−1 corresponded to CO and OC–OH, respectively [28]. Compared to CDs, GT-PCDs showed enhanced CO absorption at 1710 cm−1, reflecting new functional groups introduced during grafting. The broad absorption band at 3400 cm−1 was attributed to N–H stretching vibrations [29], while peaks at 1645 cm−1 and 1581 cm−1 (N–H bending) and 1104 cm−1 and 1040 cm−1 (C–N stretching) indicated retained amine groups [30], providing conditions for electrostatic adsorption of genes.

High-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) provided quantitative surface composition analysis (Fig. 1e–h). The C 1s spectrum revealed peaks for C–C (284.8 eV), C–O (286.3 eV), and O–CO (289.2 eV) [31–33], The O–CO in CDs originated from the surface carboxyl groups, consistent with FT-IR spectroscopy results. The O 1s spectrum of CDs displays peaks for CO (532.5 eV) and C–O (533.5 eV) [34], with GT-PCDs showing similar peaks but slightly shifted. Notably, the CO proportion in GT-PCDs is lower than in CDs, contrasting with FT-IR results. This apparent discrepancy may stem from the surface sensitivity of XPS (probing depth of approximately 5–10 nm), which prevents the detection of shielded deep-layer functional groups.

The agarose gel electrophoresis results indicate that complete loading of pDNA is achieved when the mass ratio of GT-PCDs to pDNA is 10:1 (Fig. 1i). At this ratio, GT-PCDs@pDNA exhibits extremely low cytotoxicity (Fig. S2a), thus being selected as the optimal ratio for subsequent experiments.

Given the elevated levels of ROS in the lesion sites of PD patients, we evaluated the H2O2 responsiveness of GT-PCDs@pDNA (Fig. 1j). The thioketal bonds cleavage induced by H2O2 triggered significant particle size reduction (from 38.1 nm to ∼10 nm; Fig. 1k) and surface charge reversal (from −1.7 mV to +12.0 mV; Fig. 1l). The resulting ∼10 nm nanoparticles meet the nuclear pore size threshold (∼30 nm) [35], while the cationic surface enhances nuclear membrane affinity. This dual mechanism establishes optimal conditions for subsequent nuclear gene delivery.

Serum stability was assessed through 24-h incubation in 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS), with dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis revealing minimal variations in particle size and Zeta-potential (<3 nm and <1 mV, respectively; Fig. 1m and n). Quantitative analysis demonstrated a trehalose loading capacity of 5.23 ± 0.21 % (w/w). GT-PCDs@pDNA exhibited negligible pDNA/trehalose release in saline, serum, and PBS environments (Fig. 1o and S2b), whereas H2O2 treatment induced concentration-dependent release. These findings collectively confirm the superior serum stability and ROS-triggered release capability of GT-PCDs@pDNA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MPP+-induced ROS production informs ROS-responsive GT-PCDs@pDNA design
ce:section.ce:para: The neurotoxin 1-methyl-4-phenylpyridinium (MPP+) iodide selectively targets dopaminergic neurons, triggering oxidative stress and excessive ROS production by disrupting mitochondrial function, ultimately leading to neuronal apoptosis [36]. Using the sensitive ROS fluorescent probe ROS Brite™, we monitored intracellular ROS levels in SH-SY5Y cells exposed to varying MPP + concentrations. As shown in Figs. S2b and S2c, ROS fluorescence intensity increased significantly in a dose-dependent manner, confirming that MPP + induces oxidative stress. These findings highlight a key mechanism of MPP + toxicity and offer guidance for designing ROS-responsive GT-PCDs@pDNA nanocarriers. Such systems can exploit the oxidative microenvironment to achieve targeted and efficient therapeutic drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS-triggered nuclear gene delivery in PD models using GT-PCDs@pDNA nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the potential of GT-PCDs@pDNA nanoparticles for ROS-mediated nuclear gene delivery (Fig. 2 a), we analyzed their subcellular distribution. SH-SY5Y cells, either treated with MPP+ (modeled group) or untreated (control), were incubated with GT-PCDs@pDNA. Cell nuclei were labeled with Hoechst33342 (blue fluorescence) for confocal imaging. In the modeled group, small red fluorescent spots were prominently localized within nuclei (blue regions), while larger spots remained outside. In contrast, the control group exhibited predominantly larger red spots outside the nuclei, with no notable nuclear localization. This suggests that in the absence of oxidative stress, intact GT-PCDs@pDNA nanoparticles, due to their size, fail to enter the nucleus (Fig. 2b). Furthermore, fluorescence intensity analysis revealed a near-complete overlap of GT-PCDs@pDNA and Hoechst33342 signals in the modeled group (Fig. 2c). The colocalization of pDNA-Cy5.5 with Hoechst33342 was significantly higher in the modeled group compared to controls (Fig. 2d), confirming efficient nuclear delivery under oxidative stress conditions.

To investigate the uptake dynamics, MPP + -modeled cells were incubated with GT-PCDs@pDNA for varying durations and observed using confocal microscopy. Nuclear internalization of nanoparticles increased with time (Fig. 2e–g), accompanied by a progressive reduction in particle size and a diffuse nuclear distribution at 120 min (Fig. 2e–h). This "large-to-small" transition, combined with Figs. S2c and d, suggests ROS-induced shell degradation of GT-PCDs@pDNA, releasing pDNA. This process was facilitated by changes in surface charge; Weakly negatively charged nanoparticles transformed into positively charged particles under ROS-responsive conditions, enhancing nuclear adsorption and nuclear pore entry. Additionally, the size reduction implies trehalose release in the cytoplasm. These findings demonstrate precise cytoplasmic and nuclear delivery of therapeutic trehalose and pDNA in damaged neurons.

The oxidative stress level, proportional to MPP + concentration, influenced nuclear delivery efficiency. SH-SY5Y cells treated with increasing MPP + doses showed higher pDNA nuclear delivery (Figs. S2e–g). To further confirm ROS-mediated nuclear delivery, GT-PCDs@pDNA nanoparticles were loaded with a red fluorescent protein (RFP) gene and incubated with cells under varying MPP + doses for 24 h (Fig. 2i). RFP transfection efficiency increased with both MPP + concentration and incubation time (Fig. 2j–m, S2h-j), highlighting the nanoparticles' ability to adapt delivery efficiency based on oxidative stress severity. These results suggest that GT-PCDs@pDNA nanoparticles provide a versatile platform for gene therapy in PD by enabling controlled and targeted drug release under ROS conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GT-PCDs@pDNA promotes clearance of α-syn aggregates in PD
ce:section.ce:para: Abnormal accumulation of α-syn, especially its phosphorylated form at serine 129, is a well-established biomarker for assessing α-syn pathology in PD [37]. To evaluate the therapeutic potential of GT-PCDs@pDNA, we conducted a series of experiments to investigate its effect on α-syn aggregation. Results showed that both GT-PCDs and GT-PCDs@pDNA significantly reduced the immunofluorescence intensity of α-syn compared to the MPP + -treated control group, with GT-PCDs@pDNA exhibiting a markedly stronger effect (Fig. 3 a and b).

Quantitative analysis of SNCA mRNA levels revealed significant gene silencing in cells treated with G-PCDs@pDNA and GT-PCDs@pDNA (Fig. 3c). Western blot analysis further confirmed reduced levels of α-syn monomers and, importantly, a significant decrease in α-syn aggregates in the GT-PCDs@pDNA-treated group, whereas G-PCDs@pDNA had no such effect (Fig. 3d and e). Notably, neither GT-PCDs loaded with trehalose alone nor G-PCDs@pDNA carrying only pDNA achieved the dual effect of clearing existing α-syn aggregates and inhibiting α-syn reformation (Fig. 3f). This highlights the necessity of combining trehalose and therapeutic pDNA in a single delivery system to effectively eliminate α-syn and its aggregates.

Given the critical role of autophagy dysregulation in α-syn accumulation [37], we hypothesized that GT-PCDs@pDNA might enhance autophagolysosomal clearance mechanisms. Autophagy [38], a lysosomal degradation pathway, involves the formation of autophagosomes and their fusion with lysosomes to degrade cellular debris, including α-syn aggregates. Transcription Factor EB (TFEB), a key regulator of autophagy and lysosome biogenesis [39], plays an essential role in this process. Immunofluorescence analysis revealed that GT-PCDs@pDNA significantly increased TFEB expression and nuclear translocation, while G-PCDs@pDNA showed weaker TFEB expression and no significant nuclear localization (Fig. 3g and h). This suggests that GT-PCDs@pDNA activates autophagy by enhancing TFEB-mediated lysosomal function.

Further analysis showed that GT-PCDs@pDNA treatment significantly elevated LC3-II protein levels, a marker of autophagosome formation (Fig. 3i and j) [40]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed that MPP + treatment reduced the number of autophagosomes and autolysosomes, with cargo accumulation within autophagosomes indicating impaired degradation (Fig. 3k and l). GT-PCDs@pDNA treatment restored the number of autophagosomes and autolysosomes, suggesting enhanced autophagic activity.

To confirm functional autophagic flux, we utilized SH-SY5Y cells expressing GFP-RFP-LC3. In this assay, acidic autolysosomes quench GFP signals but retain RFP fluorescence. GT-PCDs@pDNA treatment significantly increased the red-to-green fluorescence ratio, indicating enhanced autophagic flux (Fig. 3m–p). Additionally, For quantitative analyses of lysosomal morphology, we increased replicates for dimension-based parameters to account for structural heterogeneity and bolster statistical robustness, adhering to established morphological analysis methodologies [41,42]. Lysosome imaging using LAMP1 antibody revealed that the MPP + -treated group exhibited a reduction in the number of lysosomes, an increase in their diameter, and an inflammatory phenotype of perinuclear distribution [43]. GT-PCDs@pDNA restored lysosome size and number to levels comparable to wild-type cells (Fig. 3q and r). In conclusion, GT-PCDs@pDNA effectively clears α-syn aggregates in neurons by promoting TFEB nuclear localization and activating the autophagy-lysosome pathway. These findings provide novel insights into therapeutic strategies for PD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of neuroprotective effects and hemolytic properties of GT-PCDs@pDNA
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the therapeutic potential of GT-PCDs@pDNA in mitigating neuronal apoptosis, we conducted an in vitro study using SH-SY5Y cells, a well-established model for PD under MPP + stimulation [36,44]. Flow cytometry analysis (Figs. S3a and b) showed that GT-PCDs@pDNA significantly reduced MPP + -induced apoptosis, restoring cell viability to levels comparable to the normal control group. Additionally, the hemolytic properties of GT-PCDs@pDNA were evaluated in defibrinated mouse blood to ensure their safety for potential in vivo applications. Hemolysis rates remained consistently below 2 % (Figs. S3c–e), confirming their biocompatibility and supporting further animal studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GT-PCDs@pDNA enhances BBB penetration and nuclear gene delivery in PD models
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the ability of GT-PCDs@pDNA to cross the BBB, we used transwell filters with bEnd.3 cells to simulate the BBB in vitro (Fig. 4 a). After 12 h of incubation, luminescent imaging and fluorometric assays revealed that glucose-functionalized GT-PCDs@pDNA significantly enhanced transport to the lower chamber compared to non-glucose -modified nanoparticles (Fig. 4b and c). Flow cytometry and confocal imaging further confirmed nanoparticle transcytosis across the epithelial layer and subsequent uptake by SH-SY5Y cells (Fig. 4d–f), demonstrating that glucose modification facilitates efficient BBB penetration and cellular uptake.

Pharmacokinetic studies have demonstrated that the plasma half-life of Cy5.5-labeled pDNA in mice treated with GT-PCDs@pDNA is significantly extended to 47.2 min, compared to the 13.6-min plasma half-life of free pDNA (Fig. S4a). In vivo imaging revealed that GT-PCDs@pDNA rapidly accumulates in the brain within 1 h post-tail vein injection in fasted mice and persists for up to 48 h (Fig. 4h). This enhancement was attributed to Glut-1-mediated transcytosis, driven by glucose functionalization and glycemic control, leveraging the high expression of Glut-1 in the brain (Fig. 4i) [45]. In contrast, significantly weaker fluorescence was observed for free pDNA, nanoparticles without glucose modification (T-PCDs@pDNA), and non-fasted conditions, which was further confirmed by ex vivo brain imaging and fluorescence quantification (Fig. 4j and k).

To investigate ROS-triggered nuclear gene delivery in PD mouse brains, confocal imaging evaluated NPs subcellular distribution in substantia nigra sections (Fig. 4l). In PD models, Cy5.5-labeled GT-PCDs@pDNA colocalized with nuclear labeling (Fig. 4m and n), demonstrating their feasibility as nuclear gene delivery vectors. To clarify GT-PCDs@pDNA's effect on in vivo nuclear gene delivery efficiency, mice were intravenously injected with GT-PCDs@pDNA carrying an RFP gene. Elevated RFP expression was observed in MPTP-induced PD brains compared to normals (Fig. 4o–q). Since RFP expression requires nuclear pDNA entry [46], this suggests enhanced nuclear gene delivery in PD models under high ROS stimulation. Notably, particle size analysis showed reduced dimensions of GT-PCDs@pDNA in PD models, indicative of ROS-triggered degradation (Fig. 4r).

These findings suggest that GT-PCDs@pDNA efficiently crosses the BBB via Glut-1-mediated transcytosis, accumulates in the brain, and delivers genes to the nucleus under ROS stimulation. This dual functionality positions GT-PCDs@pDNA as a promising candidate for targeted gene therapy in PD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GT-PCDs@pDNA ameliorates behavioral symptoms in PD mouse models
ce:section.ce:para: Building on the promising in vitro results and effective brain-targeted delivery, we evaluated the therapeutic effects of GT-PCDs@pDNA on behavioral symptoms in MPTP-induced PD mouse models (Fig. 5 a). One week post-MPTP administration, open field test results (Fig. 5b) showed significant motor deficits in the PD group, including bradykinesia, reduced central zone entries, shorter total movement distance, slower speed, and increased rest time (Fig. 5c and d). Remarkably, GT-PCDs@pDNA treatment significantly improved these motor impairments (Fig. 5d). Similarly, in the rotarod and pole tests (Fig. 5e–g), GT-PCDs@pDNA substantially alleviated motor skill defects caused by MPTP (Fig. 5f–h). These findings demonstrate that GT-PCDs@pDNA effectively mitigates behavioral symptoms in PD mouse models, underscoring its potential as a therapeutic strategy for PD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GT-PCDs@pDNA enhances autophagy, ameliorates α-syn pathology, and safeguards dopaminergic neurons
ce:section.ce:para: The abnormal accumulation of α-syn is a pivotal mechanism in the onset and progression of PD [37]. We investigated GT-PCDs@pDNA's potential to mitigate α-syn pathology in MPTP-induced mouse neurons. Consistent with in vitro findings, GT-PCDs@pDNA treatment markedly decreased SNCA transcription and protein expression (Fig. 5i–k). Western blot analysis confirmed GT-PCDs@pDNA's capacity to restore autophagic flux in vivo, compared to G-PCDs@pDNA without trehalose loading (Fig. 5l and m). Additionally, a decreased GFP/RFP ratio in the GT-PCDs@pDNA group further supported increased autophagic flux (Figs. S4b and c) [47].

Immunofluorescence analysis revealed enhanced α-syn immunoreactivity in the substantia nigra of MPTP-modeled mice (Fig. 5n and o). Conversely, GT-PCDs@pDNA treatment reduced α-syn immunoreactivity and preserved a higher count of healthier tyrosine hydroxylase-positive (TH+) dopaminergic (DA) neurons, characterized by larger cell bodies and longer neurite outgrowth (Fig. 5n and o). Immunohistochemical analysis further demonstrated that MPTP significantly decreased TH + cell counts in the substantia nigra pars compacta (SNc) (Fig. 5p), whereas GT-PCDs@pDNA treatment significantly inhibited this reduction (Fig. 5q and r). Collectively, these results indicate that GT-PCDs@pDNA can effectively ameliorate α-syn pathology and DA neuron degeneration, thereby increasing neuronal density and alleviating PD symptoms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GT-PCDs@pDNA suppresses α-syn toxicity and neuroinflammation, restoring microglial homeostasis
ce:section.ce:para: The aggregation and propagation of toxic α-syn are known to induce neuroinflammation, exacerbating PD progression [48–51]. Microglial activation, marked by increased Iba1 expression [52], serves as a direct indicator of neuroinflammatory activity [13]. Consistent with expectations, Iba1 staining revealed a significant increase in Iba1 levels in the substantia nigra of MPTP-induced PD model mice (Fig. 6 a and b). However, GT-PCDs@pDNA treatment restored Iba1 expression to levels comparable to those in healthy controls. Morphological analysis showed that microglia in healthy mice exhibited a branched morphology with small cell bodies and fine cellular processes. In contrast, MPTP-treated mice displayed amoeboid microglia characterized by cell body hypertrophy and reduced branching. Remarkably, GT-PCDs@pDNA treatment restored microglia to a steady-state branched morphology, indicative of reduced inflammatory activation (Fig. 6a and b). Additionally, GT-PCDs@pDNA significantly increased anti-inflammatory cytokines (TGF-β, IL-10, and IL-4) while reducing proinflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-1β, and TNF-α) in PD model mice (Fig. 6c and d), thereby improving the immune microenvironment and suppressing excessive immune activation. Notably, both the GT-PCDs@pDNA-treated group and PBS control group maintained 100 % survival rates throughout the 22-day therapeutic window, with no acute or delayed mortality events observed. Additionally, the treated mice exhibited stable body weight with no significant fluctuations (Fig. S4d). Additionally, hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining of various organs indicated no organ damage (Fig. S4e), and both the blood routine parameters and biochemical indicators remained normal, confirming the absence of hematological toxicity or impairment of liver and kidney function (Fig. S4f). In summary, GT-PCDs@pDNA effectively mitigates α-synuclein–driven neuroinflammation and restores microglial homeostasis, offering a promising therapeutic strategy for neurodegenerative diseases such as PD.

In summary, this study presents a novel glycosylated nanocarrier, GT-PCDs@pDNA, which effectively targets the pathological mechanisms of PD through a dual-strategy approach. This system selectively delivers the autophagy activator trehalose to diseased neuronal tissues while transporting therapeutic pDNA into the nucleus, promoting the degradation of α-syn aggregates and inhibiting their regeneration. The nanocarrier ensures precise drug release within the high-ROS environment characteristic of PD neurons while safely crossing the BBB. Our findings demonstrate that GT-PCDs@pDNA mitigates α-syn pathology, protects dopaminergic neurons, and enhances therapeutic specificity. The specificity and targeted action of this system represent a promising step toward the development of multifunctional therapeutic approaches for PD. Future research should focus on optimizing the long-term efficacy and scalability of this nanoplatform and exploring its potential applications in other neurodegenerative diseases characterized by protein aggregation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of BBB permeability in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: To mimic the BBB in vitro, we employed the Transwell cell culture system and constructed an in vitro BBB model using mouse brain microvascular endothelial cells (bEnd.3), followed by assessment of the integrity of the cellular monolayer. After the transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) value of the bEnd.3 cell monolayer reached or exceeded 200 Ω cm2, SH-SY5Y cells were seeded on coverslips in the lower chamber for subsequent experiments. Subsequently, GT-PCDs@pDNA, T-PCDs@pDNA, or free pDNA (labeled with Cy5.5) were added to the upper chamber, and the media from both chambers were collected at 1, 2, 4, 6, and 12 h incubation time points. Fluorescence intensity was measured at a wavelength of 684 nm using a microplate reader (PerkinElmer, Enspire) to analyze the pDNA content in both chambers, thereby calculating the pDNA transport efficiency across the BBB. Finally, SH-SY5Y cells in the lower chamber were imaged using a Leica DM6 microscope, and fluorescence intensity was quantified using ImageJ software.

SH-SY5Y cells were seeded in 96-well plates and cultured to approximately 80 % confluence. Then, GT-PCDs@pDNA or control groups were added to the cells and incubated for 24 h. After incubation, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) (Meilun Biology, MB4698-1) was used according to the manufacturer's protocol, and absorbance was measured at a wavelength of 570 nm using a microplate reader (PerkinElmer, Enspire) to calculate cell viability.

We used the ROS fluorescent probe ROS Brite™ (AAT Bioquest, 16002) to detect ROS levels in SH-SY5Y cells treated with different concentrations of the modeling agent MPP+ (0, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0). Specifically, SH-SY5Y cells were seeded in culture plates and grown to approximately 80 % confluence. These cells were then incubated with different concentrations of MPP+ at 37 °C for 24 h. Staining was performed with Hoechst33342 and ROS Brite™ (10 μM), and fluorescent images were captured under a Leica DM6 microscope. Fluorescence intensity was quantified using ImageJ software.

For immunofluorescence analysis, SH-SY5Y cells were seeded on coverslips in 24-well plates. The cells, subjected to different treatments, were fixed with PBS containing 4 % paraformaldehyde and permeabilized with PBS containing 0.2 % Triton X-100 for 15 min. The cells were incubated with primary antibodies in TBS-T blocking buffer containing 5 % nonfat dry milk. The primary antibodies used included Anti-TFEB antibody (abcam, ab267351, 1:50 dilution) and pS129-α-syn (825701, Bio Legend, 1:500 dilution) Secondary antibodies tagged with Cy5 (Servicebio, 1:150 dilution) were used for visualization. Cell nuclei were stained with Hoechst33342. Images were captured using a Zeiss LSM800 confocal microscope and analyzed using ImageJ software.

For experiments involving GT-PCDs@pDNA-mediated pDNA delivery and neuronal cell protection, we used flow cytometry to measure pDNA (pGPU6/RFP, carrying the RFP sequence) delivery efficiency and cell apoptosis rate. First, cells were harvested by incubation with trypsin (Servicebio, G4011, EDTA-free), centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 4 min to form a cell precipitate, and then resuspended in 300 μL of PBS (0.01 M). Samples were run on a BD FACS Aria III flow cytometer and analyzed using FlowJo (10.8.1) software.

To detect GT-PCDs@pDNA-mediated nuclear gene delivery in vitro, GT-PCDs@pDNA (pDNA labeled with Cy5.5) was added to untreated or MPP+-treated (0, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 3 mM) SH-SY5Y cells, or incubated for different durations. Cell nuclei were stained with Hoechst33342. Fluorescent signals of pDNA and cell nuclei were captured using a Zeiss confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, LSM800), and images were analyzed for colocalization using the Profile function in ZEN Lite 3.0 software.

In vitro gene transfection experiments were conducted to assess transfection efficiency using pDNA with the RFP as a reporter gene. Specifically, transfection efficiency was reflected by detecting the percentage of RFP-positive cells (RFP+ cells). GT-PCDs@pDNA was incubated with untreated or MPP+-treated cells at different concentrations for 12, 24, and 48 h, and then imaged using a Leica DM6 microscope and analyzed by flow cytometry. Transfection efficiency was calculated as the number of RFP+ cells divided by the total number of cell nuclei and expressed as a percentage.

Neuroprotection assessment using apoptosis detection was conducted with GT-PCDs@pDNA. SH-SY5Y cells were seeded in 6-well plates and co-incubated with GT-PCDs@pDNA or control groups for 4 h, followed by treatment with 2 mM MPP+ (Sigma-Aldrich, D048) for 24 h (Control group received no treatment). After incubation, cells were stained using an Annexin-V-FITC and propidium iodide (PI) Apoptosis Kit (Elabscience, E-CK-A211) according to the manufacturer's protocol. Cells were detected and analyzed using a BD FACS Aria III flow cytometer and FlowJo (10.8.1) software (see Flow Cytometry Analysis for details).

Male C57BL/6 mice (highly sensitive to MPTP, [36]) used in this study were purchased from Southern Medical University (License No.: SCXK (Guangdong) 2021-0041). This study adhered to all relevant ethical regulations. All animal procedures followed the guidelines of the Ethics Committee for Scientific Research of South China Normal University and were approved by the committee (Approval No.: SCNU-SLS-2024-005). MPTP-induced PD mouse models were established according to previous methods [44]. Mice were intraperitoneally injected with 20 mg/kg of 1-Methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine (MPTP·HCl, Sigma-Aldrich, M0896) at a concentration of 2 mg/mL daily for 7 consecutive days; mice in the negative control group received an equal volume of saline. Drug treatment began 7 days after the last MPTP injection.

To assess the ability of GT-PCDs@pDNA to cross the BBB under blood glucose regulation, mice were divided into a fasting group (fasted for 24 h before intravenous administration) and a non-fasting group (free access to food). To verify Glut-1 targeting and effective brain delivery, glucose-modified (GT-PCDs@pDNA) and unmodified (T-PCDs@pDNA) nanoparticles were injected into the two groups, respectively. The injected GT-PCDs@pDNA or control (loaded pDNA labeled with Cy5.5) was administered via the tail vein at a dose of 1.25 mg/kg pDNA equivalent (dissolved in 200 μL of 0.01 M PBS). Thirty minutes after injection, 200 μL of 20 % glucose solution (also dissolved in 0.01 M PBS) was intraperitoneally injected. Fluorescence imaging was recorded at different time points using the Aniview (BLT Photon Technology) near-infrared imaging system. Additionally, in the organ fluorescence imaging experiment, mice were euthanized 4 h after intravenous drug administration, and hearts, livers, spleens, lungs, kidneys, and brains were collected, washed, weighed, and subjected to fluorescence imaging. These organs were homogenized and processed in 1 mL of 1 % Triton X-100 solution, followed by centrifugation for 30 min at 14,000 rpm. The fluorescence intensity of Cy5.5 in the supernatant was detected by fluorometry.

In vivo therapeutic experiments involved administering drugs to mice every three days. Mice in the MPTP-treated group and those not treated with MPTP served as positive and negative controls, respectively. Both groups only received injections of the corresponding dose of 0.01 M PBS solution to validate the pathological dysfunction in PD model mice.

GT-PCDs@pDNA and naked pDNA (equivalent to 1 mg of pDNA per kilogram of body weight, with pDNA labeled with Cy5.5) were injected into mice via the tail vein. At predetermined time points, approximately 50 μL of blood was collected from the orbital cavity of the mice. After centrifugation, the plasma was collected and diluted with PBS containing EDTA. Fluorescence intensity was measured using a microplate reader (PerkinElmer, Enspire), and sample concentrations were determined based on a prepared standard curve. Data are expressed as a percentage of the injected dose (%ID), with plasma collected at 1 min considered as the maximum injected dose (100 %ID). Nonlinear regression and two-phase decay curve fitting analysis were performed using GraphPad Prism software.

Red blood cells (RBCs) were collected by centrifuging blood collected from the orbital cavity of mice and washed three times with PBS (0.01 M) by centrifuging at 2000 rpm for 5 min. Next, the RBCs were diluted in neutral PBS to form an RBC suspension and incubated with nanoparticles at 37 °C for 1 h. Finally, the RBC suspension was centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 5 min, and 100 μL of the supernatant was used to measure the absorbance value at 540 nm using a plate reader. ddH2O and 0.01 M PBS were used as positive and negative controls, respectively.

Total RNA was extracted from cells and brain tissue using an RNA extraction kit (ESscience, RN001) and then reverse-transcribed into cDNA using a reverse transcription kit (Vazyme, R323-01). Quantitative PCR was performed on a fluorescent quantitative PCR instrument (ABI, QuantStudio 5) using SYBR Green premix (ABI, A25742). The number of mRNA copies was calculated using the 2−ΔΔCT method and normalized using the ACTB gene of humans or mice. The sequences of the qPCR primers are as follows:
SNCA.
SH-SY5Y (SNCA)
Forward primer: GGACCAGTTGGGCAAGAATGA.
Reverse primer: ATTGTCAGGATCCACAGGCA.
Mouse (SNCA)
Forward primer: CAAAGGCCAAGGAGGGAGTT.
Reverse primer: GTCACCACTGCTCCTCCAAC.
ACTB.
SH-SY5Y (ACTB)
Forward primer: CCTTCCTTCCTGGGCATGG.
Reverse primer: GATCTTCATTGTGCTGGGTGC.
Mouse (ACTB)
Forward primer: CCTAGGCACCAGGGTGTGAT.
Reverse primer: AGCACAGGGTGCTCCTCA.

Cultured cells or mouse brain tissue (substantia nigra pars compacta) were homogenized on ice for 30 min using RIPA lysis buffer containing protease/phosphatase inhibitors (Servicebio, G2002). Proteins were extracted using 1 % Triton X-100/PBS buffer. After centrifuging at 16,000g for 15 min at 4 °C, the Triton X-100 supernatant was collected. Protein concentrations were measured using the Pierce™ BCA Protein Assay Kit (Thermo Scientific, 23250). Samples (25 μg of protein) were electrophoresed on 12 % SDS-PAGE gels and then transferred to polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membranes, blocked with 5 % skimmed milk powder, and incubated with primary antibodies overnight. The primary antibodies used included anti-α-syn (610787, BD biosciences, 1:1000 dilution), pS129-α-syn (825701, Bio Legend, 1:1000 dilution), β-actin (Servicebio, GB15001, diluted 1:3000), and anti-LC3B (ab192890, Abcam, diluted 1:2000). Subsequently, the membranes were incubated with horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-labeled secondary antibodies (Servicebio, GB23303, diluted 1:1000) for 2 h. Finally, the blots were visualized using BeyoECL Star reagent (Beyotime, P0018AS) and captured using a ChemiDoc Touch (Bio-Rad, CA, USA). Protein bands were quantified using ImageJ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo ROS responsiveness of GT-PCDs@pDNA
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the ROS responsiveness of GT-PCDs@pDNA in mouse brains, the brain homogenate was centrifuged to obtain the supernatant. Briefly, mice treated with GT-PCDs@pDNA were euthanized, and PBS was perfused transcardially before extracting the intact brain. The brain tissue was then separated and homogenized to produce a brain homogenate. These single-cell suspensions were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min at 4 °C, and the clarified supernatant was diluted to 28 mL of PBS and ultracentrifuged at 150,000 rpm for 2 h at 4 °C to precipitate the nanoparticles. The precipitate was resuspended in 50 μL of PBS and centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 15 min at 4 °C to remove residual homogenate. Finally, the resuspended solution was analyzed using TEM to detect the integrity of the intact nanoparticles.

After euthanasia, mice were immediately perfused with 30 mL of PBS (0.01 M) followed by 4 % paraformaldehyde in PBS (4 % PFA/PBS) for tissue fixation. Brains were rapidly removed and further fixed in 4 % PFA/PBS at 4 °C for 24 h. For cryosectioning, fixed brains were transferred to 30 % sucrose in PBS for 48 h and then embedded in OCT compound (Servicebio G6059) for freezing. Coronal slices (50 μm) of the substantia nigra pars compacta (SNpc) were obtained using a cryostat, stored in PBS containing 0.05 % sodium azide, and kept at 4 °C for fluorescence imaging. Additionally, a portion of rapidly frozen brain samples were stored at −80 °C for RNA and protein extraction. For paraffin sectioning, fixed tissues were embedded in paraffin and cut into 5 μm-thick sections for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining or immunostaining. Prior to immunostaining, paraffin sections were dewaxed, rehydrated, and incubated in boiling 10 mM citrate buffer (pH 6.0) for 20 min for antigen retrieval.

During immunostaining, mouse brain tissue sections (SNpc) were first washed three times with PBS and then underwent antigen retrieval in citrate buffer (pH 6.0). Samples were permeabilized and blocked with PBS containing 0.3 % Triton X-100 and 5 % goat serum for 1 h at room temperature. They were then incubated overnight at 4 °C with primary antibody solutions. After washing, fluorescently labeled secondary antibodies were added and incubated for 2 h at room temperature, followed by nuclear staining with Hoechst33342 (Biosharp, BS117). For immunohistochemical analysis of TH+ dopaminergic (DA) neurons, samples were first incubated overnight with a primary antibody against tyrosine hydroxylase (anti-TH), followed by incubation with an HRP-labeled secondary antibody (Servicebio, GB23301, 1:200 dilution) for 2 h at room temperature and visualization using DAB rapid chromogenic reagent (Servicebio, G1212). Images were captured under a Leica DM6 microscope and quantified using ImageJ software. Primary antibodies included anti-α-syn (610787, BD biosciences, 1:500 dilution), anti-TH (Pel-Freeze, P40101-150, 1:500 dilution), and Iba-1 (Servicebio, GB113502, 1:200 dilution). Secondary antibodies tagged with Cy5 (Servicebio, 1:150 dilution) were used for visualization.

Brain tissue samples for direct fluorescence imaging were fixed in 4 % PFA/PBS at 4 °C for 24 h, dehydrated in 30 % sucrose for 48 h, and then embedded in OCT compound for freezing. Sections were counterstained with Hoechst33342 and images of Hoechst33342, Cy5.5-pDNA, RFP, and GFP fluorescence signals were captured at 40 × magnification using a Zeiss confocal microscope (LSM800). To quantify nuclear entry efficiency, colocalization of Hoechst33342 and Cy5.5-pDNA was analyzed using the Profile tool in ZEN Lite 3.0 software. Transfection efficiency was determined by calculating the ratio of RFP+ cells to the total number of nuclei, expressed as the percentage of RFP+ cells among total nuclei. The ratio of GFP to RFP signals was analyzed and measured using ImageJ software. After washing with TBS-T and mounting, the average intensities of GFP and RFP signals were analyzed in randomly selected fields at 40 × magnification.

Mice were individually handled for 10–20 s each day for 5 days prior to the experiment to adapt to handling procedures. For each behavioral test, mice with abnormal performance during training were excluded. Mice with similar behavioral values were selected and randomly grouped, with no mice excluded from the final analysis. On the day of the experiment, mice were transferred from the housing room to the behavioral laboratory and allowed to acclimate for 30 min before testing. Experimental methods were adapted from relevant literature [53,54].

Open Field Test: Conducted in a 50 cm × 50 cm × 50 cm apparatus, mice were allowed to freely explore for 5 min while their movement trajectories were recorded by a high-definition camera positioned above the apparatus.

Pole Test: The pole was a metal rod with a diameter of 1 cm and a length of 75 cm, wrapped in gauze bandage. Mice were trained for two days before the formal test, with three trials per day. During the test, mice were placed at the top of the pole, and the time to turn around and reach the bottom of the pole was recorded. The test was considered complete when all four paws touched the ground.

Rotarod Test: Mice were placed on a rotating rod set to accelerate from 5 to 40 rpm, and the latency to fall off was automatically recorded by the device. Model mice underwent three days of pre-training before MPTP injection, using a mode that accelerated to 40 rpm within 5 min. Their fall times were recorded one week after MPTP injection.

After treatment, dissected brain tissues were rinsed with 0.01 M PBS and supernatants were collected by centrifugation at 5000g for 5 min. Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) were performed for Interleukin-6 (IL-6, GEM0001), Interleukin-1 beta (IL-1β, GEM0002), Tumor Necrosis Factor-alpha (TNF-α, GEM0004), Transforming Growth Factor-beta (TGF-β, GEM0051), Interleukin-10 (IL-10, GEM0003), and Interleukin-4 (IL-4, GEM0008) according to the manufacturer's protocols, using reagents purchased from Servicebio. Absorbance was measured at 450 nm. Cytokine concentrations were calculated using diluted standards provided by the manufacturer, following the manufacturer's instructions.

All statistical analyses were conducted using Prism software (version 9.5.1), and the Figures were assembled in Adobe Illustrator (2020). All data are presented as mean ± SD There were at least 3 biological replicates for each condition; the number of biological replicates presented is listed in the Figure legends. Unless otherwise specified, biological replicates represent independent treatments conducted in separate culture wells or mice. All micrographs, gels, and blots are representative images from at least n = 3 independent experiments. Statistical significance was assessed using a two-tailed unpaired Student's t-test, one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), or two-way ANOVA, with appropriate follow-up tests to correct for multiple comparisons. The threshold for statistical significance was set at P < 0.05.

Limin Zhai: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yifei Gao: Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Hao Yang: Validation. Haoyuan Wang: Validation. Beining Liao: Validation, Investigation. Yuxue Cheng: Validation. Chao Liu: Validation. Jingfeng Che: Methodology. Kunwen Xia: Formal analysis. Lingkun Zhang: Supervision, Investigation. Yanqing Guan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biomaterials.2025.123345.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Drug delivery systems for RNA therapeutics
